title: Introduction
content:
S3 Security Practice In this lab, we practice security best practices for securing data in Amazon S3.
To protect your data in Amazon S3, by default, users only have access to the S3 resources they create. You can grant access to other users using one or a combination of the following access management features: AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM) to create users and manage access to their respective; Access Control Lists (ACLs) to grant user access to each object; storage policy to configure permissions for all objects in an S3 bucket and Query String Validation to grant limited-time access to others using a temporary URL. Amazon S3 also supports Audit Logs that list requests made to S3 resources so you get a complete view of the audience and the data accessed.

title: Launch CloudFormation Template
content:
Launch CloudFormation Template We perform the initialization of the resources to use for the lab.
Method 1: Download CloudFormation template
Method 2: Download CloudFormation template
Access the interface AWS Management Console
Find CloudFormation Select CloudFormation In the CloudFormation interface
Select Stack Select Create stack In the Create stack interface
Select Template is ready
Template source, depending on how you choose the template
You can choose the path Amazon S3 URL: https://aws-storage-immersion-day.s3.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/sid-base-template.yaml
Or you can also upload files.
In this section
You enter FCJ-S3-LAB for Stack name Regarding Lab Modules, you choose true for Deploy S3 Security Lab (the lab is in progress) and you can choose to add Deploy Storage Performance Lab (preparing for the next lab). Other modules choose false Select Next Select Next Confirm required IAM generation
Select I acknowledge that AWS CloudFormation might create IAM resources with custom names. Select I acknowledge that AWS CloudFormation might require the following capability: CAPABILITY_AUTO_EXPAND Select Create Stack Complete the Stack creation process
When the status of the stack changes to CREATE_COMPLETE

title: S3 Security Best Practices
content:
S3 Security Best Practices In this lab, we practice security best practices for securing data in Amazon S3.
To protect your data in Amazon S3, by default, users only have access to the S3 resources they create. You can grant access to other users using one or a combination of the following access management features: AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM) to create users and manage access their respective; Access Control Lists (ACLs) to grant user access to each object; storage policy to configure permissions for all objects in an S3 bucket and Query String Validation to grant limited-time access to others using a temporary URL . Amazon S3 also supports Audit Logs that list requests made to S3 resources so you get a complete view of the audience and the data accessed.
Content Introduction Preparation Require HTTPS Require SSE-S3 Encryption Block Public ACLs Configure S3 Block Public Access Restrict Access to a S3 VPC Endpoint Use AWS Config Rules to Detect a Public Bucket Use Amazon Access Analyzer for S3 Resource Cleanup

title: Preparation
content: Preparation Content Initialize CloudFormation Template Security Group Configuration Generate Access Keys EC2 Server Connection Bucket Name

title: Secure Network Access
content:
Safe internet access Configure Security Group for the lab.
In the AWS Management Console interface
Find VPC Select VPC In the VPC interface
Select Security Group Select SID-ssh-sg Select Actions Select Edit inbound rules Configure Inbound rules
Select Add rule Select Type HTTP, Source Anywhere-IPv4 Select Type SSH, Source MyIP (in case you can’t connect, you can change it to Anywhere-IPv4) Then select Save rules Check the configured Security Group

title: Generate access key
content:
Generate an access key for IAM user The access key is the permanent credential for the IAM user or the root user of the AWS account. You can use access keys to programmatically sign requests to the AWS CLI or AWS API (either directly or using the AWS SDK). An access key consists of two parts: an access key ID (e.g. AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE) and a secret access key (e.g. wJalrXUtnFEMI/K7MDENG/bPxRfiCYEXAMPLEKEY). As the username and password, you must use both the access key ID and the secret access key together to authenticate your requests. Manage your access keys as securely as you use your username and password.
Access the AWS Management Console interface
Find IAM Select IAM In the IAM interface
Select Users We have 2 users SID-lab-user1 and SID-lab-user2 Select user SID-lab-user1 In the SID-lab-user1 interface
Select Security credentials Select Create access key Generate access key successfully.
View key details Select Download.csv file Do the same with SID-lab-user2
Then note down the information of the 2 keys.

title: Require HTTPS
content:
Require HTTPS In this section, we need to create S3 Bucket Policy to request a connection to use HTTPS
First we access S3
Select Buckets Select sid-security-xxxxxxx bucket In the sid-security-xxxxxxx bucket interface Select Permissions In the Bucket policy section, select Edit Copy the following bucket policy into Bucket Policy Editor {"Statement": [{"Action": "s3:*","Effect": "Deny","Principal": "*","Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::BUCKET_NAME/*","Condition": {"Bool": {"aws:SecureTransport": false}}}]} Replace BUCKET_NAME with the group name you copied into your text editor. Make sure you don’t remove the /* end of the group name.
Check Policy and select Save changes Return to SSH interface to SID-security-instance. Run command aws s3api head-object --key app1/file1 --endpoint-url http://s3.amazonaws.com --profile user1 --bucket ${bucket} The command will return a 403 error because the endpoint-url is HTTP. Continue running the following command. aws s3api --endpoint-url https://s3.amazonaws.com --profile user1 head-object --key app1/file1 --bucket ${bucket} The command succeeds because you used s3api which uses HTTPS.

title: EC2 Connection
content:
Connect EC2 Access the AWS Management Console interface
Find EC2 Select EC2 In EC2 interface
Select SID-security-instance Select Connect In the Connect to instance interface
Select EC2 instance Connect Select Connect Successful connection Run the following commands to set up the AWS CLI. aws configure Then use Access key and configure. Create a credential file for use by the AWS CLI with the following command. This will allow you to easily switch between two different IAM users. cd ~/.awsen credentials Type i and press enter to enter insert mode
Now copy and paste the updated credential file from your text editor into your vi session. It will look like the following, but with your unique Access Keys. Press esc** then type :wq! and press enter to save and exit vi. You will use this SSH session throughout this lab.

title: Require SSE-S3 Encryption
content:
Require SSE-S3 Encryption Amazon S3 supports both server-side encryption (with 3 key management options: SSE-KMS, SSE-C, SSE-S3) and client-side encryption for data uploads. Amazon S3 provides flexible security features to prevent unauthorized users from accessing your data. You can connect to S3 resources from Amazon Virtual Private Cloud (Amazon VPC) using the VPC endpoint
In this section, we create S3 Bucket Policy to require encryption of data at rest.
Similar to the previous lab, we access AWS Management Console
Find S3 Select S3 In the S3 interface
Select Buckets Select sid-security-xxxxxxx bucket In the sid-security-xxxxxxx bucket interface
Select Permission
In Bucket policy, select Edit
Copy bucket policy to Bucket Policy Editor {"Statement": [{"Effect": "Deny","Principal": "*","Action": "s3:PutObject","Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::BUCKET_NAME/*","Condition": {"StringNotEquals": {"s3:x-amz-server-side-encryption": "AES256"}}}]} Replace BUCKET_NAME with the bucket name you copied into your text editor and click Save changes. Return to the SSH interface. Run command cd ~echo "123456789abcdefg" > textfile Execute the command put an object into the bucket aws s3api put-object --key text01 --body textfile --profile user1 --bucket ${bucket} The request will fail because the object is not encrypted.
Continue running put object using SSE-S3 encoding aws s3api put-object --key text01 --body textfile --server-side-encryption AES256 --profile user1 --bucket ${bucket} The command was successful because the PUT used SSE-S3.
Check S3 has 1 file text01

title: Block Public ACLs
content:
Block public ACL Same as before, go to Amazon S3
Select Bucket Select sid-security-xxxxxxx bucket In the sid-security-xxxxxxx bucket interface
Select Permission Under Bucket Policy select Edit Copy the bucket policy to Bucket Policy Editor* {"Statement": [{"Effect": "Allow","Principal": "*","Action": ["s3:PutObject","s3:PutObjectAcl"],"Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::BUCKET_NAME/*","Condition": {"StringEquals": {"s3:x-amz-acl": "private"}}}]} Replace BUCKET_NAME with the bucket name you copied into your text editor and click Save changes Go back to the SSH section and connect to SID-security-instance aws s3api put-object --key text01 --body textfile --profile user1 --bucket ${bucket} The request will succeed because the default for an ACL object is private.
Continue running the command aws s3api put-object --key text01 --body textfile --acl public-read --profile user1 --bucket ${bucket} This command also succeeds, but it is not the behavior one would expect
Current Group Policy allows private ACLs but does NOT DELETE anything. It is important to write policies that prevent actions, not allow them when trying to restrict actions against a group. The current group policy also allows Public access to unintentional groups because the main character is a wildcard.
Access to S3
Select buckets Select sid-security-xx bucket Select Permissions Select Edit Bucket policy Replace new policy {"Statement": [{"Effect": "Deny","Principal": "*","Action": ["s3:PutObject","s3:PutObjectAcl"],"Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::BUCKET_NAME/*","Condition": {"StringEquals": {"s3:x-amz-acl": ["public-read","public-read-write","authenticated-read"]}}}]} Select Save changes Edit the policy successfully. Back to the SSH interface, run the following command. aws s3api put-object --key text01 --body textfile --profile user1 --bucket ${bucket} The request will succeed because the default for an ACL object is private.
Continue to run the following command. aws s3api put-object --key text01 --body textfile --acl public-read --profile user1 --bucket ${bucket} The request failed because group policy now restricts the ACL from being read publicly.

title: Bucket Name
content:
Amazon S3 Bucket Name Access AWS Management Console
Find S3 Select S3 In the S3 interface
Select Buckets Select sid-security-xxxxxxx bucket View details of the sid-security-xxxxxxx bucket interface
Make a note of the bucket name

title: Configuring S3 Block Public Access
content:
Configure S3 Block Public Access With just a few selects in the S3 management console, you can apply S3 Block Public Access to any bucket in your account – both existing buckets and any buckets any new ones created in the future – while ensuring that there is no public access to any objects. S3 Block Public Access settings replace the S3 permission for public access, making it easy for account administrators to set up centralized controls to prevent changes in security configurations, no matter how the added object or how to create a storage area.
Practice Access to S3
Select sid-security-xxxxxxxx Select Permissions
In the Bucket Policy section, select Delete Fill in Delete and select Delete to confirm the deletion. In the Block public access (bucket settings) section
Select Edit Make edits
Select Block public access to buckets and objects granted through new access control lists (ACLs) Select Save changes Fill in confirm and then select Confirm After the change, we observe that Block all public access is On Return to SSH interface aws s3api put-object --key text01 --body textfile --profile user1 --bucket ${bucket} The request succeeds because default for an ACL object is private.
Continue running the command aws s3api put-object --key text01 --body textfile --acl public-read --profile user1 --bucket ${bucket} The request failed because group policy restricts the ACL from being read publicly.
Perform access to S3
Select Buckets Select sid-security-xxx bucket In the exchange bucket
Select Permissions For Block public access (bucket settings), select Edit Make edits
Select Block public access to buckets and objects granted through new access control lists (ACLs) Select Save changes Fill in confirm and select Confirm Finish editing.

title: Restrict access to S3 VPC Endpoint
content:
Restrict access to S3 VPC Endpoint Access AWS Management Console
Find VPC Select VPC In the VPC interface
Select Enpoints Select Create endpoint In the Create endpoint interface
Name tag, enter S3-Endpoint Select AWS services In services, we find S3
Select Type Gateway Select VPC SID-vpc, do not select Route Table Select Create endpoint Successfully created VPC endpoint
Note down Endpoint ID for the next steps Access to S3 bucket
Select sid-security-xxx bucket In the bucket interface
Select Permissions Select Edit Bucket Policy. Copy the bucket policy into the Bucket Policy Editor {"Statement": [{"Action": "s3:*","Effect": "Deny","Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::BUCKET_NAME/*","Condition": {"StringNotEquals": {"aws:sourceVpce": "VPC_ENDPOINT_ID"}},"Principal": "*"}]} - Replace your **BUCKET NAME** and **VPC ENDPOINT ID**.Return to SSH interface aws s3api head-object --key app1/file1 --profile user1 --bucket ${bucket} Return to VPC endpoint interface
Select S3 endpoint Select Actions Select Manage route tables In the Manage route tables interface
Select SID-routes Select Modify route tables Successfully added route table Return to SSH interface aws s3api head-object --key app1/file1 --profile user1 --bucket ${bucket}

title: Using AWS Config to Detect Public Bucket
content:
Using AWS Config to Detect Public Bucket Go to AWS Management Console
Find AWS Config Select AWS Config In the AWS Config interface
Select Rules Select Add rules Choose a rule type
Select Add AWS managed rule For AWS Managed Rules
Find s3-bucket-public-read-prohibited Select s3-bucket-public-read-prohibited Select Next Select Next Check again and select Add rule The rule has been added successfully. Go to AWS Config interface and select Dashboard
Return to S3 bucket interface
Select sid-security-xxx bucket. In the bucket interface select Permissions For Access control list (ACL), select Edit In the ACL editing interface
For Everyone (public access), select List and Read Select I understand the effects…* and select Save changes Now we will see Permissions in state Public (Public Bucket) Return to Config interface
Select Rules Select s3-bucket-public-read-prohibited Observation Resource in scope is currently not available. In the same interface, select Actions
Select Re-evaluate Observe the Resource in scope section appears resource.

title: Using Amazon Access Analyzer for S3
content:
Using Amazon Access Analyzer for S3 Go to AWS Management Console
Find IAM Select IAM In the IAM interface
Select Access analyzer Select Create analyzer In the Create analyzer interface
Select Create analyzer Create Analyzer successfully. In the Analyzer interface
Select Active Select S3 bucket Access to Access analyzer for S3 interface In the S3 interface
Select Bucket Select sid-security-xxx In the bucket interface
Select Permissions For Access control list (ACL)
Select Edit In the Edit ACLs interface
Uncheck List and Read of Everyone (public access) Select Save changes Return to Access Analyzer interface of IAM
Select Resolved Find S3 Select Finding ID appears. In the Finding ID interface
View status is in Resolved

title: Clean up resources
content: Clean up resources Delete S3 bucket Access to S3 interface Select Bucket related to the lab Select Delete bucket (note to Empty bucket before deleting) Verify the bucket to be deleted. Select Delete Clear CloudFormation Stack Access the interface CloudFormation Select Stack Select the stacks related to the lab. Select Delete Confirm stack deletion.
